expanded_description: Le Châtelier's Principle predicts how equilibrium systems respond to temperature and pressure changes. Unlike concentration changes, temperature changes alter the equilibrium constant ($K_{\text{eq}}$) itself, while pressure changes (for gases) shift the equilibrium position without changing $K_{\text{eq}}$. Understanding these effects is crucial for optimizing industrial processes (like ammonia synthesis), predicting reaction behavior under varying conditions, and distinguishing between factors that change equilibrium position versus those that change the equilibrium constant.

core_concept.principle: Temperature Changes Alter $K_{\text{eq}}$; Pressure Changes Shift Position (Gases Only)
core_concept.explanation: Temperature changes affect the thermodynamics of the reaction, changing the value of $K_{\text{eq}}$ (exothermic: $T \uparrow \rightarrow K \downarrow$; endothermic: $T \uparrow \rightarrow K \uparrow$). Pressure changes (via volume changes) shift the equilibrium position to counteract the pressure change (toward fewer gas moles if pressure increases) but do not change $K_{\text{eq}}$ at constant temperature. Adding inert gases at constant volume has no effect on equilibrium position.

image.alt: Diagram showing temperature and pressure effects on equilibrium
image.caption: Temperature: changes $K_{\text{eq}}$; Pressure (gases): shifts position, $K_{\text{eq}}$ constant.

sections.introduction: Temperature is the only stress that changes the value of the equilibrium constant $K_{\text{eq}}$. This is because temperature affects the relative stability of reactants and products (thermodynamics). To predict the effect of temperature changes, treat heat as either a reactant (endothermic) or a product (exothermic). Le Châtelier's Principle then applies: adding heat shifts equilibrium to consume heat; removing heat shifts equilibrium to produce heat.

key_concept.definition: Temperature changes alter $K_{\text{eq}}$. For exothermic reactions ($\Delta H < 0$), increasing temperature decreases $K_{\text{eq}}$ (shifts left). For endothermic reactions ($\Delta H > 0$), increasing temperature increases $K_{\text{eq}}$ (shifts right).
key_concept.context: Unlike concentration or pressure changes, temperature changes fundamentally alter the equilibrium constant because they change the relative free energies of reactants and products. This distinction is critical: concentration/pressure shifts restore the original $K_{\text{eq}}$; temperature changes establish a new $K_{\text{eq}}$.

image.alt: Diagram showing Keq changes with temperature for exo/endothermic reactions
image.caption: Exothermic: $T \uparrow \rightarrow K \downarrow$; Endothermic: $T \uparrow \rightarrow K \uparrow$.

principles.principle: Treat Heat as a Reactant or Product
principles.statement: To predict temperature effects, include heat in the chemical equation: exothermic reactions have heat as a product; endothermic reactions have heat as a reactant.
principles.application: This analogy allows direct application of Le Châtelier's Principle for concentration changes to temperature changes. Increasing temperature = adding heat; decreasing temperature = removing heat. The system shifts to consume added heat or replace removed heat.
principles.analogy: Think of heat like a chemical species: if heat is on the product side (exothermic), adding heat (increasing $T$) pushes the reaction left (toward reactants). If heat is on the reactant side (endothermic), adding heat pushes the reaction right (toward products).
principles.example: Exothermic: $\text{N}_2 + 3\text{H}_2 \rightleftharpoons 2\text{NH}_3 + \text{heat}$. Increase $T$ (add heat) → shift left (consume heat). Endothermic: $\text{N}_2\text{O}_4 + \text{heat} \rightleftharpoons 2\text{NO}_2$. Increase $T$ (add heat) → shift right (consume heat).

principles.statement: Unlike concentration or pressure changes, temperature changes modify the equilibrium constant $K_{\text{eq}}$ itself, not just the equilibrium position.
principles.application: For exothermic reactions, $K_{\text{eq}}$ decreases as $T$ increases (products become less favored). For endothermic reactions, $K_{\text{eq}}$ increases as $T$ increases (products become more favored). This is quantified by the van't Hoff equation, though qualitative predictions suffice for Le Châtelier analysis.
principles.analogy: Concentration/pressure changes are like moving along a seesaw (position changes, but the balance point $K_{\text{eq}}$ stays the same). Temperature changes are like moving the fulcrum of the seesaw (the balance point $K_{\text{eq}}$ itself changes).
principles.example: Haber process (exothermic): At $25^\circ\text{C}$, $K_{\text{eq}}$ is large (favors $\text{NH}_3$). At $450^\circ\text{C}$, $K_{\text{eq}}$ is smaller (favors reactants more). Higher $T$ reduces yield thermodynamically, but is used for kinetic reasons (faster rate).

components.definition: Key temperature quantities: $\Delta H$ = enthalpy change (exothermic $< 0$, endothermic $> 0$), $K_{\text{eq}}$ = equilibrium constant (changes with $T$), shift direction = toward reactants or products.
components.equation: $\text{Exothermic } (\Delta H < 0): \uparrow T \rightarrow \text{shift left}, K \downarrow \quad \text{Endothermic } (\Delta H > 0): \uparrow T \rightarrow \text{shift right}, K \uparrow$
components.interpretation: Temperature effect depends on reaction enthalpy sign. Increasing $T$ favors the endothermic direction (absorbs heat). Decreasing $T$ favors the exothermic direction (releases heat).

image.caption: Temperature components: treat heat as reactant (endothermic) or product (exothermic).

worked_examples.title: Predicting Temperature Effect on Equilibrium
worked_examples.problem: For $2\text{SO}_2(g) + \text{O}_2(g) \rightleftharpoons 2\text{SO}_3(g)$, $\Delta H = -198 \text{ kJ/mol}$ (exothermic). Predict the effect of (a) increasing temperature, (b) decreasing temperature on equilibrium position and $K_{\text{eq}}$.

step_by_step.explanation: Identify reaction type based on $\Delta H$.
step_by_step.calculation: $\Delta H < 0$ → Exothermic. Heat is a product: $2\text{SO}_2 + \text{O}_2 \rightleftharpoons 2\text{SO}_3 + \text{heat}$.

step_by_step.explanation: Analyze effect of increasing temperature (part a).
step_by_step.calculation: Increase $T$ = add heat (product). System shifts left (toward reactants) to consume excess heat. $K_{\text{eq}}$ decreases.

step_by_step.explanation: Analyze effect of decreasing temperature (part b).
step_by_step.calculation: Decrease $T$ = remove heat (product). System shifts right (toward products) to replace removed heat. $K_{\text{eq}}$ increases.

worked_examples.final_answer: (a) Increase $T$: shift left (toward $\text{SO}_2$ and $\text{O}_2$), $K_{\text{eq}}$ decreases. (b) Decrease $T$: shift right (toward $\text{SO}_3$), $K_{\text{eq}}$ increases. This demonstrates application of Le Châtelier's Principle to temperature changes for exothermic reactions.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Temperature effect: exothermic + increase $T$ → shift left, $K$ decreases; endothermic + increase $T$ → shift right, $K$ increases.

worked_examples.title: Temperature Effect on Endothermic Reaction
worked_examples.problem: For $\text{N}_2\text{O}_4(g) \rightleftharpoons 2\text{NO}_2(g)$, $\Delta H = +58 \text{ kJ/mol}$ (endothermic). How does increasing temperature affect the color of the equilibrium mixture? ($\text{N}_2\text{O}_4$ is colorless, $\text{NO}_2$ is brown).

step_by_step.explanation: Identify reaction type based on $\Delta H$.
step_by_step.calculation: $\Delta H > 0$ → Endothermic. Heat is a reactant: $\text{heat} + \text{N}_2\text{O}_4 \rightleftharpoons 2\text{NO}_2$.
Increase $T$ = add heat (reactant). System shifts right (toward products) to consume excess heat.
Shift right → more $\text{NO}_2$ (brown) forms → mixture becomes darker brown.

worked_examples.final_answer: Increasing temperature shifts equilibrium right (toward brown $\text{NO}_2$), so the mixture becomes darker brown. $K_{\text{eq}}$ increases. This demonstrates temperature effect on endothermic reactions and observable physical properties.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Endothermic + increase $T$ → shift right (toward products), $K$ increases. Observable change: color intensifies toward product color.

sections.introduction: Pressure and volume changes affect equilibrium positions only for gas-phase reactions where the number of moles of gas differs between reactants and products ($\Delta n_{\text{gas}} \neq 0$). Changing pressure by changing volume alters concentrations/partial pressures, causing a shift to counteract the pressure change. Importantly, pressure changes do not alter $K_{\text{eq}}$ (at constant temperature)—only the equilibrium position shifts.

categories.description: Increasing pressure by decreasing volume shifts equilibrium toward the side with fewer moles of gas
categories.detailed_mechanism: Decreasing volume increases all gas concentrations/partial pressures. To counteract this increase, the system shifts toward the side with fewer gas molecules (fewer moles occupy less volume, reducing pressure). If both sides have equal gas moles, pressure changes have no effect on position.

examples.explanation: Reaction: $\text{N}_2(g) + 3\text{H}_2(g) \rightleftharpoons 2\text{NH}_3(g)$. Reactants: 4 mol gas; Products: 2 mol gas. Increase pressure → shift right (toward 2 mol) to reduce pressure. Application: Haber process uses high pressure (200 atm) to maximize $\text{NH}_3$ yield.

image.alt: Diagram showing shift toward fewer gas moles when pressure increases
image.caption: Pressure increase: shift toward side with fewer gas moles to reduce pressure.

examples.explanation: Reaction: $\text{H}_2(g) + \text{I}_2(g) \rightleftharpoons 2\text{HI}(g)$. Reactants: 2 mol gas; Products: 2 mol gas. Increase pressure → no shift (equal gas moles). Pressure changes don't affect equilibrium position when $\Delta n_{\text{gas}} = 0$.
examples.equation: $\Delta n_{\text{gas}} = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{pressure changes have no effect on equilibrium position}$

categories.description: Decreasing pressure by increasing volume shifts equilibrium toward the side with more moles of gas
categories.detailed_mechanism: Increasing volume decreases all gas concentrations/partial pressures. To counteract this decrease, the system shifts toward the side with more gas molecules (more moles occupy more volume, increasing pressure). This is the opposite of pressure increase.

examples.explanation: Reaction: $\text{N}_2\text{O}_4(g) \rightleftharpoons 2\text{NO}_2(g)$. Reactants: 1 mol gas; Products: 2 mol gas. Decrease pressure (increase volume) → shift right (toward 2 mol) to increase pressure. Result: more $\text{NO}_2$ forms.
examples.equation: $\text{Decrease } P \text{ (increase } V) \rightarrow \text{shift toward more gas moles}$

categories.description: Adding an inert gas (e.g., He, Ar) has different effects depending on whether volume or pressure is held constant
categories.detailed_mechanism: At constant volume: adding inert gas increases total pressure but doesn't change partial pressures of reacting gases → no shift. At constant pressure: adding inert gas increases volume → decreases partial pressures of reacting gases → shifts toward more gas moles (same as pressure decrease).

examples.explanation: Reaction: $\text{N}_2 + 3\text{H}_2 \rightleftharpoons 2\text{NH}_3$ in rigid container. Add He gas. Total pressure increases, but $P_{\text{N}_2}$, $P_{\text{H}_2}$, $P_{\text{NH}_3}$ unchanged → $Q$ unchanged → no shift.
Reaction in piston (constant $P$). Add He gas. Volume must increase to maintain $P$. Partial pressures of reacting gases decrease → shift toward more gas moles.
examples.equation: $\text{Constant } P + \text{inert gas} \rightarrow \text{shift toward more gas moles}$

sections.key_concept: Pressure/Volume effects: (1) Increase $P$ (decrease $V$) → shift toward fewer gas moles; (2) Decrease $P$ (increase $V$) → shift toward more gas moles; (3) $\Delta n_{\text{gas}} = 0$ → no effect; (4) Inert gas at constant $V$ → no effect; (5) $K_{\text{eq}}$ unchanged (constant $T$).

sections.introduction: Certain scenarios involving temperature and pressure changes are frequently misunderstood. Solids and liquids don't count for pressure effects. Catalysts don't shift equilibrium. Distinguishing between position shifts and $K_{\text{eq}}$ changes is critical. This section clarifies these special cases to prevent common errors in equilibrium analysis.

categories.type: Solids and Liquids in Pressure Changes
categories.description: Pure solids and liquids don't count when determining moles of gas for pressure effects
categories.detailed_mechanism: Pressure changes affect gas concentrations significantly (gases are compressible). Solids and liquids are essentially incompressible, so their concentrations don't change with pressure. Only count gas-phase species when comparing moles for Le Châtelier pressure predictions.

examples.explanation: Reaction: $\text{CaCO}_3(s) \rightleftharpoons \text{CaO}(s) + \text{CO}_2(g)$. Reactants: 0 mol gas; Products: 1 mol gas. Increase pressure → shift left (toward 0 mol gas). Solids are ignored in mole count.
examples.equation: $\text{Count only gas moles for pressure effects}$

categories.description: Adding a catalyst speeds up both forward and reverse rates equally, causing no equilibrium shift
categories.detailed_mechanism: Catalysts lower activation energy for both directions equally. Since $K_{\text{eq}} = k_f/k_r$, the ratio remains unchanged. Catalysts only speed up attainment of equilibrium; they don't change the equilibrium position or $K_{\text{eq}}$ value.

examples.explanation: Haber process uses iron catalyst. Effect: reaches equilibrium faster at $450^\circ\text{C}$. Equilibrium yield: unchanged (determined by $T$ and $P$ via Le Châtelier). Catalyst makes process economically viable by reducing time, not by changing yield.

categories.description: Only temperature changes $K_{\text{eq}}$; concentration and pressure changes shift position but keep $K_{\text{eq}}$ constant
categories.detailed_mechanism: This is a critical distinction. Concentration/pressure changes make $Q \neq K_{\text{eq}}$, causing a shift until $Q = K_{\text{eq}}$ again (same $K$). Temperature changes alter the value of $K_{\text{eq}}$ itself, establishing a new equilibrium constant.

examples.explanation: Add $\text{N}_2$ to Haber process: shift right, but $K_{\text{eq}}$ same (at constant $T$). Increase $T$ for Haber process (exothermic): shift left, and $K_{\text{eq}}$ decreases. Only $T$ changes the constant.
examples.equation: $\text{Concentration/Pressure: shift position, } K \text{ constant}; \text{ Temperature: shift position, } K \text{ changes}$

sections.key_concept: Special cases: (1) Count only gas moles for pressure effects (ignore solids/liquids); (2) Catalysts = no shift, only faster rate; (3) Only temperature changes $K_{\text{eq}}$; concentration/pressure shift position but $K_{\text{eq}}$ constant.

sections.section_title: Industrial Applications: Optimizing Temperature and Pressure
sections.introduction: Industrial chemical processes actively manipulate temperature and pressure based on Le Châtelier's Principle to maximize product yields while balancing kinetic and economic constraints. Understanding these applications demonstrates the practical importance of equilibrium principles in real-world chemical engineering.

components.key_feature: Le Châtelier applications: (1) High pressure (200 atm) → shift right (4 mol → 2 mol gas); (2) Moderate temperature ($450^\circ\text{C}$) → balance equilibrium yield (exothermic, favors low $T$) with reaction rate (favors high $T$); (3) Iron catalyst → faster equilibrium attainment (no position change); (4) Continuous $\text{NH}_3$ removal → shift right.

Le Châtelier applications: (1) Moderate pressure (1-2 atm) → slight shift right (3 mol → 2 mol gas); (2) Moderate temperature ($450^\circ\text{C}$) → balance yield (exothermic, favors low $T$) with rate (favors high $T$); (3) $\text{V}_2\text{O}_5$ catalyst → faster rate; (4) Excess $\text{O}_2$ → shift right.

components.function: Maximize NO yield for nitric acid synthesis
components.key_feature: Le Châtelier applications: (1) High temperature ($800-900^\circ\text{C}$) → despite exothermic reaction, high $T$ needed for practical rate (kinetics dominates); (2) Platinum-rhodium catalyst → enables reaction at lower $T$ than uncatalyzed; (3) Continuous product removal → shift right.

sections.section_title: Practice Problems: Temperature and Pressure Effects
sections.introduction: Apply your understanding of temperature and pressure effects on equilibrium to these practice problems. Work through predictions of shifts, $K_{\text{eq}}$ changes, and industrial optimization strategies to build proficiency in applying Le Châtelier's Principle.

worked_examples.title: Combined Temperature and Pressure Analysis
worked_examples.problem: For $\text{PCl}_5(g) \rightleftharpoons \text{PCl}_3(g) + \text{Cl}_2(g)$, $\Delta H = +92 \text{ kJ/mol}$ (endothermic), predict the effect of each change on equilibrium position and $K_{\text{eq}}$: (a) increase temperature, (b) increase pressure, (c) add catalyst, (d) remove $\text{Cl}_2$.

step_by_step.explanation: Analyze effect of increasing temperature (part a).
step_by_step.calculation: Endothermic ($\Delta H > 0$), heat is reactant. Increase $T$ (add heat) → shift right (toward products). $K_{\text{eq}}$ increases (endothermic + increase $T$).

step_by_step.explanation: Analyze effect of increasing pressure (part b).
step_by_step.calculation: Reactant side: 1 mol gas; Product side: 2 mol gas. Increase pressure → shift toward fewer gas moles → shift left (toward $\text{PCl}_5$). $K_{\text{eq}}$ unchanged (constant $T$).

step_by_step.explanation: Analyze effect of adding catalyst (part c).
step_by_step.calculation: Catalyst increases both forward and reverse rates equally → no shift in equilibrium position. $K_{\text{eq}}$ unchanged.
Remove product ($\text{Cl}_2$) → shift right (toward products) to replace removed product. $K_{\text{eq}}$ unchanged (constant $T$).

worked_examples.final_answer: (a) Shift right, $K_{\text{eq}}$ increases; (b) Shift left, $K_{\text{eq}}$ unchanged; (c) No shift, $K_{\text{eq}}$ unchanged; (d) Shift right, $K_{\text{eq}}$ unchanged. This demonstrates combined application of Le Châtelier's Principle to multiple stress types, distinguishing between position shifts and $K_{\text{eq}}$ changes.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Le Châtelier's Principle applications: temperature (changes $K_{\text{eq}}$), pressure (shifts position, $K_{\text{eq}}$ constant), catalyst (no shift), concentration (shifts position, $K_{\text{eq}}$ constant).

worked_examples.title: Industrial Process Optimization
worked_examples.problem: For the exothermic reaction $2\text{NO}(g) + \text{O}_2(g) \rightleftharpoons 2\text{NO}_2(g)$, suggest conditions to maximize $\text{NO}_2$ yield using Le Châtelier's Principle. Consider temperature, pressure, and concentration strategies.

step_by_step.calculation: Exothermic ($\Delta H < 0$) → low temperature favors products (shift right). However, too low $T$ slows reaction rate. Strategy: moderate temperature to balance yield (thermodynamics) with rate (kinetics).

Reactant side: 3 mol gas (2 NO + 1 $\text{O}_2$); Product side: 2 mol gas ($\text{NO}_2$). High pressure → shift toward fewer gas moles → shift right (toward $\text{NO}_2$). Strategy: use high pressure to maximize yield.

Add reactants ($\text{NO}$ or $\text{O}_2$) → shift right. Remove product ($\text{NO}_2$) continuously → shift right to replace removed product. Strategy: use excess $\text{O}_2$ (cheap) and continuously remove $\text{NO}_2$.
Catalyst doesn't change equilibrium position but speeds up attainment of equilibrium. Strategy: use catalyst to reach equilibrium faster at moderate temperature.

worked_examples.final_answer: Optimal conditions: (1) Moderate temperature (balance yield with rate); (2) High pressure (shifts toward fewer gas moles, favors $\text{NO}_2$); (3) Excess $\text{O}_2$ (shifts right); (4) Continuous $\text{NO}_2$ removal (shifts right); (5) Catalyst (faster equilibrium attainment). This demonstrates comprehensive application of Le Châtelier's Principle to industrial process optimization.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Process optimization: combine multiple Le Châtelier strategies (temperature, pressure, concentration, product removal) to maximize yield while considering thermodynamic favorability with kinetic and economic constraints.

worked_examples.title: Identifying $K_{\text{eq}}$ Changes
worked_examples.problem: For $\text{N}_2(g) + 3\text{H}_2(g) \rightleftharpoons 2\text{NH}_3(g)$, $\Delta H = -92 \text{ kJ/mol}$, which of the following changes will alter the value of $K_{\text{eq}}$: (a) adding $\text{N}_2$, (b) increasing pressure, (c) increasing temperature, (d) adding a catalyst?

step_by_step.calculation: Concentration change → shifts equilibrium position but $K_{\text{eq}}$ remains constant (at constant $T$).

step_by_step.explanation: Analyze effect of increasing pressure (part b).
step_by_step.calculation: Pressure change → shifts equilibrium position but $K_{\text{eq}}$ remains constant (at constant $T$).

step_by_step.explanation: Analyze effect of increasing temperature (part c).
step_by_step.calculation: Temperature change → alters $K_{\text{eq}}$ value. Exothermic reaction: increase $T$ → $K_{\text{eq}}$ decreases.

step_by_step.explanation: Analyze effect of adding catalyst (part d).
step_by_step.calculation: Catalyst → no shift in position, no change in $K_{\text{eq}}$.

worked_examples.final_answer: Only (c) increasing temperature will alter the value of $K_{\text{eq}}$. Concentration, pressure, and catalyst changes shift equilibrium position but do not change $K_{\text{eq}}$ at constant temperature. This demonstrates the critical distinction between factors that change $K_{\text{eq}}$ (temperature only) versus those that shift position (concentration, pressure).
worked_examples.concept_applied: $K_{\text{eq}}$ constancy: only temperature changes $K_{\text{eq}}$; concentration/pressure/catalyst shift position but keep $K_{\text{eq}}$ constant (at constant $T$).

sections.section_title: Summary Checklist for Temperature and Pressure Effects
sections.introduction: A quick-reference checklist consolidates the key concepts for applying Le Châtelier's Principle to temperature and pressure changes.

components.composition: Determine if reaction is exothermic ($\Delta H < 0$) or endothermic ($\Delta H > 0$)
components.function: Determines temperature effect direction and $K_{\text{eq}}$ change
components.key_feature: Exothermic: heat is product; Endothermic: heat is reactant

components.composition: Count moles of gas on reactant and product sides (ignore solids/liquids)
components.key_feature: $\Delta n_{\text{gas}} \neq 0$: pressure affects position; $\Delta n_{\text{gas}} = 0$: pressure has no effect

components.composition: Exothermic + increase $T$ → shift left, $K \downarrow$; Endothermic + increase $T$ → shift right, $K \uparrow$
Increase $P$ → shift toward fewer gas moles; Decrease $P$ → shift toward more gas moles
Catalyst = no shift; Inert gas at constant $V$ = no shift; Solids/liquids ignored for pressure
components.function: Prevents common errors in equilibrium analysis
components.key_feature: Catalysts speed rate but don't shift equilibrium; inert gas effect depends on constant $V$ vs. constant $P$

components.part: Step 6: Distinguish $K_{\text{eq}}$ Changes from Position Shifts
components.composition: Temperature changes $K_{\text{eq}}$; concentration/pressure shift position but $K_{\text{eq}}$ constant
components.function: Ensures accurate interpretation of equilibrium changes
components.key_feature: Critical distinction: position shift (restore $Q = K$) vs. $K$ change (new equilibrium constant)

summary: Le Châtelier's Principle predicts equilibrium responses to temperature and pressure changes. Temperature: (1) Exothermic ($\Delta H < 0$) + increase $T$ → shift left (toward reactants), $K_{\text{eq}}$ decreases; (2) Endothermic ($\Delta H > 0$) + increase $T$ → shift right (toward products), $K_{\text{eq}}$ increases. Temperature is the only factor that changes $K_{\text{eq}}$. Pressure/Volume (gases only): (1) Increase $P$ (decrease $V$) → shift toward fewer gas moles; (2) Decrease $P$ (increase $V$) → shift toward more gas moles; (3) $\Delta n_{\text{gas}} = 0$ → no effect; (4) Inert gas at constant $V$ → no effect. Pressure changes shift equilibrium position but don't change $K_{\text{eq}}$ (at constant $T$). Special cases: catalysts don't shift equilibrium (only speed attainment); solids/liquids don't count for pressure effects. Industrial applications (Haber, Contact processes) combine multiple Le Châtelier strategies (temperature, pressure, concentration, product removal) to maximize yields while balancing thermodynamic favorability with kinetic and economic constraints. Common misconceptions: expecting catalysts to shift equilibrium, thinking pressure changes $K_{\text{eq}}$, ignoring gas mole counts, confusing position shifts with $K_{\text{eq}}$ changes. Mastery requires distinguishing factors that change $K_{\text{eq}}$ (temperature only) from those that shift position (concentration, pressure), correctly counting gas moles, and applying principles to optimize industrial processes.
